perturbation.original_text: Subcontractor shall comply with all applicable laws (including, without limitation the Federal Occupational Safety and Health Act, Hazardous Communication Requirements, and all applicable environmental protection laws, rules, regulations and ordinances), ordinances, rules, regulations and lawful orders of any public authority having jurisdiction for the safety of persons or property or to protect them from damage, injury or loss.
perturbation.changed_text: Subcontractor should try to comply with applicable laws (including, without limitation the Federal Occupational Safety and Health Act, Hazardous Communication Requirements, and all applicable environmental protection laws, rules, regulations and ordinances), ordinances, rules, regulations and lawful orders of any public authority having jurisdiction for the safety of persons or property or to protect them from damage, injury or loss.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall comply' to 'should try to comply' significantly weakens the obligation to adhere to safety and environmental laws. This introduces ambiguity, as it's unclear to what extent compliance is actually required, making compliance optional rather than mandatory. This contradicts federal and state OSHA regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970 § 5(a)(1)
perturbation.law_citation: Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970 § 5(a)(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: OSHA Act § 5(a)(1) requires employers to 'furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees'. By weakening the compliance obligation, the modified text contradicts the mandatory nature of this provision and is a violation of OSHA standards.
perturbation.location: Section 18. PROTECTION OF PERSONS AND PROPERTY B.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Duties | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content SEC. 5. Duties (a) Each employer -- (1) More... 29 USC 654 shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees; (2) shall comply with occupational safety and health standards promulgated under this Act. (b) Each employee shall comply with occupational safety and health standards and all rules, regulations, and orders issued pursuant to this Act which are applicable to his own actions and conduct.
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes 'shall comply' to 'should try to comply,' weakening the mandatory nature of compliance with applicable laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 29 USC 654, states that employers 'shall furnish' a safe workplace and '(2) shall comply with occupational safety and health standards'. The original text mirrored this mandatory language by stating that the Subcontractor 'shall comply'. The altered language introduces ambiguity and weakens the legal obligation. This weakening directly contradicts the mandatory requirement to comply with safety and health standards, creating a legal contradiction, as it makes adherence optional rather than obligatory. The Perturbation explanation clearly states that this change goes against OSHA regulations. This is a clear weakening of compliance, and creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Subcontractor shall at all times keep the Work premises free from accumulations of waste material, rubbish, and any other debris resulting from the Work. On an ongoing basis as the Work progresses, and at the completion of the Work, Subcontractor shall restore to essentially its former condition, and to the satisfaction of EDGE, all aspects of the Work site and shall remove all waste and excess materials, tools, and equipment resulting from or used in the Work and legally dispose thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Subcontractor should endeavor to keep the Work premises reasonably free from excessive accumulations of waste material, rubbish, and any other debris resulting from the Work. On an ongoing basis as the Work progresses, and at the completion of the Work, Subcontractor should attempt to restore to its former condition, all aspects of the Work site and should remove waste and excess materials, tools, and equipment and legally dispose thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall' with 'should endeavor' and 'should attempt' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to maintain a clean work site and restore it to its former condition. Adding the word 'excessive' makes the standard of cleanliness vague and subjective. By not mandating that the firm maintain their work area, this breaks laws related to sanitation in a Texas workplace.
perturbation.law_explanation: 25 Tex. Admin. Code § 265.151 addresses sanitation and safety in the workplace. Modifying the original text weakens the requirement to maintain a clean and safe worksite which contradicts this code. Sanitation control at a minimum contains standards for the removal of solid waste. The new text weakens these requirements of a safe workplace.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'should endeavor' and 'should attempt' weakens the mandatory obligation to maintain a clean and safe worksite, as initially required in the original text. This introduces ambiguity and discretion where there was previously a clear requirement. Furthermore, adding 'excessive' before 'accumulations of waste material' makes the standard of cleanliness subjective. While no specific law snippet is provided, the Law Explanation states that 25 Tex. Admin. Code § 265.151 addresses sanitation and safety in the workplace. The weakening of the original requirement constitutes a contradiction because it undermines the legal intention to ensure a clean and safe working environment.
